text: ALARIA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Tripoli, 10 m. N of Tortosa, on the coast.

ALARO, a town of Majorca, 9 m. NNE of Palma. There is an ancient castle here, and a quarry of fine marble.—Also a river of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, rising near Vatrizzia, and flowing into the Ionian sea between Santo Fili and Rocella.

ALA SHEHR. See ALAH SHAHIR. ALASHKA, ALASKA, or ALYASKA, a long peninsula, on the NW coast of N. America, running out in a SW direction from the continent of Russian America into Behring's sea, between the bay of Bristol on the NW and N, and Cook's inlet on the S and SE. I ALA It chiefly consists of a ridge of steep rocks, which in some parts attain a great elevation; and has an average breadth of 15 m.; while its length, if measured from the head of Cook's inlet, exceeds 400 m. That division of the Aleutian chain, called the Fox islands, commences at its western extremity, a very narrow channel here dividing the volcanic island of Unimakwhich rises to an alt. of 8,083 ft.—from the extremity of the peninsula. This great uninterrupted sea-wall —as it may be termed—with its insular continuation in the Aleutian range, prevents the ice brought from the Arctic ocean and down the large rivers which discharge themselves into the sea of Behring, from flowing into the N Pacific ocean; while the accumulation of ice in the former sea absorbs a large proportion of the heat of the atmosphere. Hence navigators, in passing from the Pacific into the sea of Behring, seldom fail to remark a great change of temperature; and while all the coast to the SE of the peninsula is clad with dense forests of noble timber, on that to the NW only a few stunted shrubs are seen. The island of Kadiak, sheltered from the Arctic influences by the peninsula, has an abundance of timber; while the Aleutian islands, though several degrees further S, have not a single tree; and the difference in the animal is as striking as in the vegetable world on the two sides of this peninsula. On the one side we find the walrus,-on the other, the humming-bird; and while the ice-fox is often met with on the Aleutian islands, it has never been encountered at Kadiak. The inhabitants of the peninsula call themselves Konia, Korenga, and Kagataya-Kunga.
ALASHAN, a mountain range of Mongolia, on the frontier of the Chinese prov. of Ninghiafou, and extending in a semicircular form SE as far as the Yellow river.
ALASHOUR, a town of Algiers, 32 m. S by W of Bona, near the r. bank of the Sebus.

ALASEI, or Alaseia, a large river of Siberia, rising in N lat. 67°, on a range of wooded hills called the Alasei, which branches from the main chain of the Stanovoi ridge in about N lat. 61° 30', E long. 147°, and runs up, through the Jakuti country, till it sinks into the Tundra or moss level on the shores of the Arctic ocean, forming the dividing ridge between the waters of the Indigirka on the W, and the Alasei on the E. The A. river receives a number of tributaries, some of which flow from the mountains, and others from different large lakes. Its general course is N, but it winds much, and empties itself into the sea by five arms, of which the eastern, called Lagoshkin, has a rapid current, and forms at its entrance an island 2 versts in length. It is 150 fath. broad 2 versts above the island. The next arm, 3½ versts to the W of the former, is called the Alaseiskoj Protok. The other three arms, going westwards, are sometimes mere dry channels.
ALASS. See ALLASS.

ALASSIO, a port of the Sardinia states, 6 m. SSW of Albenga, on the coast of the Mediterranean, in a small bay lying between Point Coscione on the NE, and Cape Dell' Melle on the SW. Pop. 5,000. It is protected by three small forts. The SE wind blows into the bay and the current generally sets to the SW; but there is good anchorage 1 m. from the shore in from 10 to 15 fath. Coral is fished here.

ALASSONA, or Alasonah, a kadilik and town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Romelia, and sanjak of Tirhala, on the S side of Mount Volutza, 22 m. NNW of Lorissa, on the Saranta river. Pop. 3,000. It is a place of considerable trade, and is much frequented during the great fair held in the beginning of August.

ALATA, a small "brisk stream" [Bruce] of Abyssinia, in the district of Afferawanet, which, passing a considerable v. of the same name, flows by a SSW course into the l. bank of the Abai or Nile, 2 m. to the W of the bridge of Zamócha, above which the Abai forms a magnificent fall known as the cataract of Alata. "The river gliding through a fine grassy plain, between a line of dark foliage on each side, its smooth surface disturbed by only a few ripples, falls at once perpendicularly over the ledge of rock which crosses its course, and is lost to sight, whilst in its place arise volumes of vapour having precisely the appearance of smoke." [Beke.] The depth of the fall is supposed by Beke to be about 100 ft.; but he did not measure it, and Bruce says it is "nearer 40 ft. than any other measure." When Bruce visited it, "the river," he says, "had been considerably increased by rains, and fell in one sheet of water, without any interval, above half an English mile in breadth." The Scottish traveller appears to have been deeply impressed by his visit to this fall. He says: "It was a most magnificent sight, that ages added to the greatest length of human life would not efface or eradicate from my memory." The two German doctors, Beke and Ruppell, write more soberly of it. Immediately below this fall, the Abai rushes, in a series of foaming cascades, through a narrow cleft in the rocks, above 60 ft. deep, the sides of which are in many places scarcely 2 fathoms asunder, and at about 100 ft. above the bridge approach to within 9 ft. of each other. The hills lying immediately over the banks of the river, at this gorge, are "wild rent masses of volcanic rock, partly overgrown with large trees and rampant shrubs." [Ruppell.] The bridge is 450 ft. in length, with a breadth of 15 ft. It consists of 8 arches of different sizes, of which the largest and northernmost, or that on the Biégemider side, springs at once from the rocks, and crosses the cleft; the other 7, on the Miécha side, are therefore only approach-arches. Between this bridge and that of Andabiet—a distance of 30 m. —the river has a descent of at least 2,000 ft.; its bed must therefore present a succession of falls and rapids nearly throughout the whole distance.
ALATA, a v. on the W coast of Corsica, 4 m. N of Ajaccio.

ALATAMAHA, or ALTAMAHA, a river of Georgia, U.S., formed by the union of the Ockmulgee and the Oconee, under the parallel of 32° N. These main branches rise under the 34th parallel, and both pursue SSE courses at an average distance of about 45 m. from each other. After the junction of its headstreams, the A. becomes a large river flowing with a gentle current through forests and plains, in a SE direction, and by a course of about 100 m. into a bay of the Atlantic on the E coast of Georgia, to which it gives name, between St. Simon's and Sapello islands, and 60 m. SW of Savannah. It is navigable, on the Oconee branch, by boats of 30 tons, to Dublin, a point 300 m. above its mouth; and by steam-boats to Milledgeville. Its western or Ockmulgee branch is also navigable to the same distance from the sea; but the navigation of both rivers is chiefly carried on in large flat-bottomed boats, on account of the shoals and rapids. The whole length of the river to its extreme sources, under the 34th parallel, exceeds 500 m. The bar at its mouth has 14 ft. at low water. The tidal flow is 25 m. The S channel, or sound of A., which is the largest and deepest entrance, is 500 yards wide at its confluence with the Atlantic.

ALATRI, a town of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, delegation of Frosinone, 45 m. ESE of Rome, near the Neapolitan frontier. It is the see of a bishop, and possesses a cathedral and several churches; also some supposed Pelasgic remains. Pop. 9,000. It is the ancient Alatrium.

ALATYR, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Simbirsk, at the junction of the Alatyr and Sura, 120 m. | ALA WNW of Simbirsk. Pop. 3,000. The surrounding country is thickly wooded.—The A. river rises to the SE of Ardatof, and joins the Sura coming from Pensa, after a course of 150 m. from W to E.

ALAUSI, a town and district of Ecuador, in the dep. of Assuay, 140 m. S of Quito, on a small river flowing into the gulf of Guyaquil. The general elevation of the valley of A. is 8,000 ft. above the level of the sea. A transverse ridge of the Andes separates it from that of Cuença; the lofty peak of Chimborazo rises on the W; and that of Cotopaxi on the E. ALAUTA, ALUTA, ALT, or OLT, a river of Transylvania and Wallachia, and one of the most important affluents of the Danube. It rises in the Carpathian mountains, on the SW frontiers of Moldavia, near the source of the Marosh; runs S for about 60 m. to a point about 12 m. NE of Kronstadt, where it turns W, then NW, and then N by W, till it touches the parallel of 45° N. near Hopecz; then turns WSW, and SW by W, till it reaches a point 16 m. SSE of Hermannstadt, where it receives the Czsibin, and turns S; it then bursts through the pass of the Rothenthurm, and enters the great plain of Wallachia, through which its course is to the E of S, past Slatina, to Turna, where it falls into the l. bank of the Danube opposite Nikopoli. Its total length is about 300 m., of which somewhat more than one-half are within Transylvania. Its navigation is difficult at present, but is capable of being greatly facilitated.

ALAVA, one of the three Basque prov. of Spain, the southern portion or apex of the triangular shaped mountainous district forming the Bascongádas Provincias, of which the base rests on the Mediterranean. It is bounded by Biscay and Guipuscoa on the N; by Navarre on the E; by Soria on the S; and by Burgos on the W. The great Cantabrian ridge, rising in the Sierra de Orduna to 5,000 ft., divides it from Biscay and Guipuscoa, and sends out a side-range which divides it from Navarre. From this latter range, two branches run out to the W, and, crossing the prov., terminate on the banks of the Ebro, which divides it from Burgos. The principal river is the Zadorra, flowing, in a course which bends round from NW to SW, to the Ebro. The next are the Ayuda, a branch of the Zadorra, and the Omecillo a branch of the Ebro; but there are a great number of minor streams and torrents, affluents of these rivers. The climate is serene and healthy, but much warmer than on the N of the Cantabrian ridge, as there is no sea-breeze here to cool the air. The mountains are clothed with forests which are of importance to the Spanish navy; and they are said to abound in iron-ore. The valleys are fertile, the soil being composed of a rich dark loam; corn is exported from this prov.; maize, flax, hemp, and oil, are extensively produced, and some wine of good quality is made. The superficies is estimated at 1,093 sq. m. The census of 1833 returned a pop. of 67,523; but M. Moreau de Jonnès estimated the pop. in 1837 at 92,807, or 84 to the sq. m., while Guipuscoa had 216, and Biscay 113 to the sq. m.; and the average density of pop. for the kingdom was 78 to the sq. m. These statistics are, however, by no means to be depended upon.—A. has one city, Vittoria; 72 towns and villages; and 434 townships. The chief towns next to the cap. are Salvatierra, Lequiano, and Gamboa. The whole prov. is divided into 6 quadrillas, and 53 hermandades, each of which sends a deputy to the junta which meets twice a-year at Vittoria. By the charter of Alonzo granted in 1332, this prov. cannot be taxed by the Crown; but it contributes to the royal exchequer a certain sum as a 'donativo voluntario;' and the code framed at Rivabellosa in 1467 is the governing code of laws, customs, and fueros. The language is the Vizcaino dialect of the Basque.—When the Cortes in 1822 divided the country into 51 prov., this prov. received the name of Vittoria; but the old name was again recognised in the royal decrees of 1837.

ALAVERDI, a town of Russian Georgia, in the district of the Inner Kakheti, 9 m. NNW of Telaf. It is the seat of the Greek bishop of Kakheti. ALAYA, ALAYEH, or ALANIEH, a city of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Karamania. It is built upon a rocky promontory, projecting into the sea, and "separated from the adjacent mountains by a broad plain, and a low sandy isthmus, from which it abruptly rises. Two of its sides are cliffs of great height, and absolutely perpendicular; and the eastern side, on which the town is placed, is so steep that the houses seem to rest on each other." [Beaufort.] Its geographical position is N lat. 36° 32', E long. 32° 2'; 7 leagues NNW from Selinty. It is a poor place, and has neither harbour nor pier; but there is tolerably good anchorage from 2 to 4 cables length ESE from the octagonal tower marked a in the subjoined sketch from Capt. Beaufort's valuable little volume on Karamania. In the middle of the sea-front are some large vaulted structures bb intended for sheltering galleys. The cliffs of A. are of a compact white limestone tinged by a red drip.—A. is said to have taken its name from its founder Alah-ed-din, son of Kaikosru, and was at one time the principal maritime fortress and naval arsenal of the Iconian dynasty. It is now an insignificant place in point of trade, and does not contain above 2,000 inhabitants. Capt. Beaufort identifies it with the Coracesium of Strabo. In modern charts the promontory appears under the name of CAPE BALDO OF UBALDO.
ALAYOR, a town and district of Minorca, 12 m. NW of Port Mahon. The town is situated on a hill. Its houses are well-built, but the streets are narrow and crooked .- St. Sauveur.

ALB, or ALP (DIE RAUHE), or the SWABIAN ALB, a branch or northern prolongation of the Schwarzwald or Black Forest mountains, uniting them to the Fichtelgebirge; running from SW to NE through the SE part of Wurtemberg, and separating the basins of the Neckar and the Danube. It is above 70 m. in length; and its breadth varies from 10 to 15 m. Its NW side presents a rugged and sudden descent; but on the SE side it gradually sinks into low undulating hills. Its highest points are the Ober Wohenberg near Bahlingen, 3,160 ft.; the Schafberg near Rosswangen, 3,121 ft.; and the Plattenberg near Bahlingen, 3,100 ft. Though not so elevated as the Schwarzwald, it is bleaker and colder. There are several extensive caverns in this range, which is calcareous, and affords several kinds of marble. The forests are chiefly beech. Large flocks of sheep are reared upon it.

ALB, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, on the l. bank of the Inn, and on the great road through the valley of the Inn to Tyrol.—Also two rivers in Baden, one of which, distinguished as the Upper Alb, descending from the Feldsee, falls into the Rhine near Allbruck, after a course of 30 m. from N to S. The other, or Lower Alb, rises in Wurtemberg, in the Dobel mountain, and, running from SE to NE, flows into the Rhine near Knielingen.

ALBA, a mountainous prov. of Sardinia; bounded on the NE and E by the prov. of Alessandria; on the S and SW by that of Mondovi; on the W by Saluzzo; and on the NW by the division of Turin. Area 145 sq. m. Pop. in 1821, 105,737. It is subdivided into 12 'giudicature' or administrative districts: viz., Alba, Bossolasco, Cornegliano d'Alba, Morra, Diano d'Alba, Cortemiglia, Bra, S. Stefano Balbo, Canale, Monforte, Govone, and Sommariva del Bosco. The principal streams are the Tanaro, the Belbo, and the Bermuda. Wheat, maize, wine, fruits, truffles, and silk, are the principal productions of the soil. Few cattle are reared.—The town of A., the capital of the prov., is situated on the r. bank of the Tanaro, at the confluence of the Tarasco, 30 m. SSE of Turin. It is the seat of a bishop, a suffragan of Turin; and has a cathedral, 3 parish-churches, and a royal college. Pop. 8,000. The ancient Alba Pompeia, or Alba Docilia, is identified with this town, which was the birth-place of the emperor Pertinax, and the painter Alladio. - There is a town of the same name in the Abruzzo Ulteriore Secundo, at the S foot of Monte Vellino, which was devastated by an earthquake in 1783; but has begun to revive, and at present has a pop. of about 4,000. It has been identified with the ancient Alba Marsorum, or Alba Fucentia.
ALBA JULIA. See Carlsburg.
ALBA REGALIS. See Weissenburg.

ALBA DE TORMES, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Salamanca, on a river of the same name. It contains 300 houses, and 7 convents, together with a castle in which, says Townsend, is deposited the armour of all the dukes of Alba, who take their title from this place. The Spaniards were defeated by the French in the neighbourhood of A. in 1809.

ALBACETE', formerly called CETEDE, a town of Spain, in Murcia, 34 m. WNW of Almanza. It is pleasantly situated in an extensive plain, at an alt. of 2,278 ft. above sea-level; and the road from Valencia to Madrid has a continued ascent of several miles from this place to a still higher plateau which belongs to the plain of La Mancha. The pop. of the town and partido is 11,500.—The administrative division of A. comprises the partidos of Albacetè, Alcaráz, Almanza, Hellin, Chinchilla, Casas-Ibanez, La Roda, and Yeste; which are subdivided into 118 pueblos or communes. Pop. in 1834, 190,326. The district is fertile, and produces wheat, barley, wine, fruit, and large quantities of saffron. An important cattle-fair is annually held at A. in September; and some indifferent cutlery is manufactured in the town.

ALBA DE YELTES, a v. of Spain, in the prov. of Salamanca, on the Yeltes river, 12 m. ENE of Ciudad Rodrigo. ALBA'GH, ALI BAUG, or ALBA'K, a mountainous district of Asiatic Turkey, in Kurdistan, on the Persian frontier, at the sources of the Zab. Alt. 7,500 ft. above sea-level. It is inhabited by Mahommedan Kurds of the Suni sect, and a considerable body of Christians of the Nestorian creed; and is tributary to the khan of the Hirki and Hirkari Kurds. Profiting by the natural defences and difficult approaches of their country, these Kurds have maintained their independence both of Turkey and Persia. Their country is of course little known. The zealous German traveller M. Schultz fell a sacrifice to his desire to explore it in the autumn of 1829; and Colonel Monteith narrowly escaped a like fate in the pass leading to Julamerck. The Christians—who are said to amount to 40,000 families-profess to be the descendants of those who fled from the persecution of Justinian, and at one time inhabited the whole country from Rumia to Bitlis. They have now separated themselves from the Mahommedan portion of the community, and have taken refuge in the impregnable fastnesses of the Jidda Dagh. They are governed by a hereditary priest, to whom each family pays a tribute of 1s. 6d. "As far as I could observe," says Colonel Monteith, "they are simple, brave, and virtuous,-far superior in character to the other Christians in Asia. I never saw," he adds, "a country so cut by deep and difficult ravines, over which they have bridges composed of two trees joined together and slung at one end; guards are placed at every passage, and signal-posts to call the people together in case of danger. They possess mines of lead, copper, and iron, which they work with great skill. Their mountains are covered with trees, amongst which cedars grow to a great size."
ALBAIDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valentia, 7 m. S of Jativa. Pop. 3,500.

ALBAN (SAINT), a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. and 19 m. ESE of Alby. The pop. of the cant. in 1831 was 7,848; of the com. and v. in 1842, 696.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Côtes-du-Nord, cant. of Pleneuf. Pop. 1,378.— Also a com. in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Verpillière. Pop. 1,050.—Also a com. and town in the dep. of Lozère, arrond. of Marrejols. Pop. 2,470.
ALBAN-D'AY (SAINT), a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, arrond. of Tournon. Pop. 1,211.
ALBAN-DES-HURTIERES (SAINT), a com. and v. of Sardinia, 6 m. SW of Agnabella, near the Arc. Pop. 1,200. Iron is wrought here.
ALBAN-DES-VILLARDS (SAINT), a com. and v. of Sardinia, 8 m. W by N of Maurienne. Pop. 1,250. Iron is also wrought here.
ALBAN-SOUS-SAMPZON, a com. of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, arrond. of Largentière. Pop. 880.
ALBANELLA, a town of Naples, in Principato Citra, 10 m. NNE of Capaccio, in a fertile but unhealthy valley. Pop. 1,800.

ALBANIA, a country of European Turkey, stretching along the E coast of the Adriatic; and bounded on the NW by the Austrian territory of Lower Dalmatia; on the N by Bosnia and Servia; on the E and SE by Romelia; on the S by Epirus; and on the W by the Ionian sea and the Adriatic. If the name be extended to Epirus and the territories of the late Ali Pasha, the southern frontier must be extended to the gulf of Arta. Its extreme N point is Mount Dormitor in the Dinarian Alps, a little to the S of the parallel of 43° N, and in 19° 14' E long. Its extreme S point—if Epirus be excluded—is in Mount Mutzkeli, at the sources of the river Argiro, in N lat. 39° 45', E long. 20° 35'. Between these two points, a line running NNW and SSE will have a length of about 230 m. Its extreme W point is nearly on the latter parallel, and in 21° 23' E long. But its outline is so irregular, and its surface so mountainous, that any estimate of its superficial area can present only an approximation to accuracy. Perhaps we shall not be wide of the truth if we estimate the superficial area of the whole country, excluding Epirus, at 18,000 sq. m. General Divisions.] Count Karaczay, whose geographical account of Albania is the best we possess, divides the country into three regions: Northern, Central, and Southern Albania. The NW portion of NORTHERN ALBANIA is formed by the district of Montenegro proper, called by the Turks, Karatagh, and by the natives Zernagora,—all which names signify 'the Black mountains.' This district is an elevated plateau of 2,000 ft. above sealevel, with a steep declivity towards the W; its eastern border approaches the W banks of the Zenta and the Moratsha; and its southern extremity skirts the lake of Skutari. Its highest summits reach an alt. of ALB 5,000 ft. The Turks have never succeeded in establishing their sway over this district, but it is supposed that Russia exercises considerable influence over the Montenegrins, through the medium of their princebishop.—The adjacent region on the E and NE, constituting the basins of the upper courses of the Zenta and the Moratsha rivers, and bounded on the N and NW by the great chain of the Dinarian Alps, is inhabited by several independent tribes of mountaineers, the allies of the Zernagorzi, and distinguished in the confederation of the Montenegrini by the name of Berdiani. Their vladika, or Greek bishop, unites the supreme political and ecclesiastical power in his own person.—The pashalik of Skutari has its NW point in Mount Kom; and its irregular northern frontier formed by different branches of the Dinarian Alps, which divide it from the Herzegovine and Bosnia, contains the sources of the Lum river, running NW to the Danube. On the NE and E, it is bounded by Bosnia and Servia. On the S, it has the sanjaks of Prissend and Elbassan in Central Albania. The lakes of Skutari on the SW, and of Plava on the N, the lower portion of the basin of the Moratsha and the Drin, and the upper portion of the basin of the White Drin, belong to this division of A. The plain of Skutari, extending along the eastern shores of the lake, and along the Moratsha river to the confluence of the Zenta, with an average width of 5 or 6 m., is the most fertile district in A., producing all the kinds of grain cultivated in Italy, with the exception of rice; and all the fruits of Southern Europe. The forests afford shelter to wild cattle, bears, wild hogs, deer, mountain-goats, lynxes, wild cats, and foxes. The number of Turks settled in this district is greater in proportion to its extent than in any other part of A.

CENTRAL ALBANIA, or that portion of the country which lies S of the Drin, and N of the Skombi, is politically divided into the sanjak of Ebassan on the E, comprising the sea-coast from the mouth of the Drin to that of the Skombi, and the basin of the Madia; the sanjak of Prissend, comprising the lower part of the basins of the White Drin and the Kara Drin, and the upper portion of their united streams; and the sanjak of Ochrida, comprising the great lake of Ochrida, the upper part of the basin of the Kara Drin which flows N from that lake, the middle portion of the basin of the Skombi, and the upper part of the basin of the Lum. On the W, the sanjaks of Prissend and Ochrida are divided from the Romelian sanjaks of Skopia or Uskub, and Monastir, by a chain of mountains stretching S from the Sharra Tagh under the parallel of 42° 5′ N, which are covered with snow the greater part of the year. Another chain running parallel to that just described, on the W side of the valley of the Kara Drin, occupies a base of 10 or 12 m. in width, and sends off two lofty ridges 30 m. distant from each other, across the sanjak of Elbassan towards the Adriatic. The basin of the Matt or Madia, and its tributaries, lies between these ridges. The upper valleys of Central A. have a rude climate, and their inhabitants chiefly depend on their cattle, sheep, and goats for subsistence. Lower down, maize is rather extensively grown, and peaches and quinces are abundant. The principal tribes are the Catholic Christian tribe of the Miriditi Albanians, and the Mahommedan tribe of the Madiani. The former are governed by native princes, and are said to exceed 18,000 in number. The littoral of Southern Albania lies between Cape Redoni and Cape Linguetta, and comprises the sanjak of Avlona, and the northern part of Janina, which latter forms the SE corner of the region. The Romelian sanjak of Monastir bounds it on the E, and Janina and Epirus on the S. The basins of the Lum and of the Voyussa form the great physical divisions of the territory; which is traversed by a mountain-range called Kandair, which branches off from the great Pindus range near the parallel of 40° 30' N, and further to the S by the Khimera or Acroceraunian range. The coast presents a level tract in some parts 10 m. in breadth. The inhabitants of this division of A. are Mahommedans and Greek Christians. Political Divisions, and Towns.] Before the rebellion of Ali Pasha of Janina, the southern districts of Albania were united to the vizirat of Epirus; and the remainder formed the ejalet or vizirat of Skutari. The present pashalik of Skutari comprehends: 1. The kadilik of Karatagh or Montenegro; and the kadilik of Antivari, whose capital of the same name, near the mouth of the Richanatz, has a pop. of 2,500. 2. The kadilik of Dulcigno, whose cap. of the same name, to the S of Antivari, has a pop. of 7,000 or 8,000. 3. The kadilik of Skutari, lying around the lake of that name, whose cap. is said to have a pop. of 40,000; while the old town of Drivast or Drisht, 4 m. to the NE of Skutari, has a pop. of 1,000. 5. The kadilik of Podgorizza, extending over the Berdas, and the adjacent mountain-regions. The town of P., on the 1. bank of the Moratsha, has 6,000 inhabitants. Zabliak, at the influx of the Moratsha into the lake of Skutari, has 1,000; and Spush or Ispush, on the Zenta, 2,000. 6. The kadilik of Alessio, comprehending the southern part of the pashalik. Its capital of the same name is on the l. bank of the Drin, not far from its mouth.—The sanjak of Dukajin extends over the NE portion of Albania, including the greater part of the plain of Metoya. It comprises: 1. The kadilik of Dukajin, with the decayed town of that name. 2. The kadilik of Petsh or Ipek, so called from its cap., the present seat of the pasha of Dukajin, a large town on the Bistrizza river, with a pop. of 12,000. 3. The kadilik of Jacova, whose cap. of the same name, on the Ervenik river, has a pop. of 18,000. —The sanjak of Prissend comprises part of the plain of Metoya, and a large mountain-tract lying around the Sharra Dagh. The town of P. on the Ricka, about 4 m. from its confluence with the Drin, has a pop. of from 15,000 to 20,000.—In the sanjak of Elbassan are the towns of Elbassan on the Skombi, with 5,000 inhabitants; Dúrazzo, on the coast, with from 9,000 to 10,000; Pekin, on the Uskomobin, with 700; Kavaya, near the coast, with from 9,000 to 10,000; and Tirana, on the Jacon, with 10,000.—In the sanjak of Ochrida is the town of that name, on the northern bank of the lake of Ochrida, with a pop. of about 1,000; Great Dibre with 8,000 and Little Dibre with 2,800, in the valley of the Kara Drin; and Croya or Akhissar, on the Ismo, with 15,000.—The sanjak of Avlona, forming the southern part of Albania, has the important town of Berat on the Chervesta, with between 8,000 and 10,000 inhabitants; Avlona, on the coast, with an equal pop.; Argiro, on the Ergir, with 4,000; and Klissura, in the valley of the Voyussa, with 1,500. Commerce and Trade.] A. exports timber, oil, wool, wheat, maize, tobacco, valonia, kermes, leeches, sheep, goats, cattle, and horses. The grain exported is chiefly Indian corn, of which, under Ali Pasha, upwards of 50 cargoes were annually sent to the Ionian islands, Italy, and Malta. Fine timber is procured close to the shore; and during the Revolutionary war, a French agent was stationed at Arta to contract for supplies of it to the marine arsenals of France. Skutari also largely exports timber, and has some yards on its lake for building vessels. Oaktimber of the best quality abounds on the mountains close upon the Dalmatian shore. Tobacco is cultivated chiefly in Upper Albania. Cotton and cottonyarn, received through Thessaly, were at one time exported to the German and Italian ports of the Adri- ~~~~~<del>~</del> ALBANIA. atic. The British garrison at Corfu receive their supplies of cattle from the eastern districts of A. bordering on Romelia, and from Northern A. The only manufactured goods exported are capotes, a kind of large white woollen great coat, with loose open sleeves, and a hood which hangs in a square piece behind; gun and pistol-stocks mounted with chased silver; velvets, stuffs, and cloths. The imports consist of coffee and sugar from Trieste; sword blades, gun-barrels, and cutlery from Venice; gold and silver thread for embroidery from Vienna; coarse cloths from Leipsic; and linen, gunpowder, and iron wares. The Bocca di Cattaro, belonging to Austria, has a good harbour, which forms the principal inlet and outlet of trade for the independent tribes of Montenegro and the Berdas. Avlona has a well-sheltered port at which goods for Berat are imported. Durazzo is also well-situated for commerce. Prevesa, on the N side of the gulf of Arta, was formerly the entrepot of Epirus. The general import and export trade of A. is conducted under the British, Ionian, Greek, and Ottoman flags.—None of the roads in A. are practicable throughout for wheeled vehicles, and goods are chiefly conveyed by pack-horses; but in dry weather, an army not encumbered with heavy artillery could march over the country. The most important lines of commercial intercourse between Albania and the Herzegovine, Servia, Romelia, and Epirus, are as follow: 1. The road leading from Skutari, along the Moratsha and the upper valley of the Zenta, and across the low range called Planinizza, into the plain of Nixitshi, where it branches off to Jezero and Piva in Bosnia, to Gasco, and to Grohovo. 2. From Podgorizza a road leads across the Dinarian Alps to Ipek by the upper valley of the Ak Drin; from Ipek, a road, passing through Istok, leads to Yenibazar in Servia; while the towns of Ipek, Jacova, and Prissend, are connected by a road running through the plains of Metoya. 3. From Jacova, a road leads to Pristina in Servia, whence there is a road practicable for carriages to Uskop or Skopia in Romelia, through the famous pass of Katshanik; while a more direct road to Uskop crosses the Sharra Tagh, and passes through the town of Kalkandelen. 4. Skutari is connected with Prissend by a road passing through a hilly country to Mied on the Drin, and thence eastwards over the Kerubi range. 5. From Antivari to Skutari a road runs along the Boyana. 6. From Skutari to Alessio, the road crosses the Drinoss, and turning S runs between the Boyana and the Drin. 7. The road from Alessio to Berat is rarely above 5 or 6 m. distant from the shores of the Adriatic; and there is a road from Alessio to Toli Monastir, which branches off from the coast-road at the Hismo river; runs up that river to Kroya, where it turns S to Torana and Elbassan; from Elbassan runs up the valley of the Skombi to Kukusa, thence to Usturga on the Kara Drin, and thence to Ochrida; between Ochrida and Tesnia crosses the Pindus chain by a circuitous route; and from Tesnia falls SE to Toli Monastir. 8. From Berat a road leads up the valley of the Chervesta and across the Pindus range to Koriji, forming the most direct line of commercial communication between Darazzo and Salonichi. 9. Another road from Berat leads to Janina or Joannina in Epirus. After crossing the Chervesta, it penetrates the mountain-mass between that river and the Voyussa; it then ascends to the town of Peramiti, whence it again descends to the banks of the Voyussa. At 20 m. from Peramiti, it leaves the valley of the river, and ascends by the v. of Ostaniji, the crest of the Khimera range; and proceeding southwards passes Kuniji on the l. and the v. of Rovenia, and then descends into the undulating plain on which Janina is built. Population.] Of the pop. of A. various estimates have been made. The standing army of their great leader Scanderberg consisted of 8,000 horse and 7,000 foot; and from this it has been calculated that the total pop, including that of Epirus, might amount to 1,400,000. This, however, it is evident, is only a very rude approximation to a census. Count Karaczaywhose guidance we have almost exclusively followed in the preceding part of this article-estimates some of the tribes as follows: 56,000 The republic of Montenegro, The Berdas, including the inhabitants of the plain of Prava, of the Berda Moratshka and Rovatzka, and the Berda Piperska and Kutshka, . The Ksella Catholics, A native Albanian, calling himself prince of the tribes of the Wassevitches, in a paper read before the Geographical Society of Paris in 1841, estimates the population of Upper Albania at 320,000, whom he classifies thus: 70,000 Independent Montenegrins, The Berda or Holmia, composed of seven indepen- 42,000 dent but federal tribes, The Uskopy or Uskotzy, a mixture of Sclavonians and Albanians on the frontiers of the indepen- Taking the pop. of Albania Proper at 1,000,000, a considerable proportion are Greeks and Turks; but the mass are of Arnaut descent. "With regard to the origin of this people, who lay as it were dormant so many centuries, during the incursions of the barbarians by whom at various times the Eastern empire was overrun, history," says Mr. Hughes, " is quite silent; all is left to conjecture; and conjecture itself has in this instance but slight grounds for the formation of an hypothesis. Their language being entirely oral, no written documents can remain to aid us in our researches. In the opinion of Col. Leake-than whom few persons are better able to judge-they are the descendants of some ancient Illyrian nation, preserved by their mountain-barriers from the intermixture of Goths, Huns, Sclavonians, and other invading tribes who settled in the country And the only hypothesis which seems able to stand against this, refers them to an emigration of Asiatic Albanians, concerning whom Strabo has left us a copious narrative, wherein some traits of character curiously coincide with these Europeans, modified by the inventions of modern science and the introduction of different religious rites." They regard themselves as the descendants of Illir, a son of Cadmus, king of Phœnicia. "The features of the Albanian,-his narrow forehead, keen grey eye, small mouth, thin arched eyebrow, high cheek bones, and pointed chin,-strongly mark a Scythian physiognomy." [Hughes.] His neck is long, his chest full and broad; and his general bearing easy, erect, and majestic to a degree which never fails to surprise the traveller. Their language, by the best judges is considered a dialect of the Sclavonic, with an intermixture of many other tongues. It is not a written language; but Major Leake has formed a grammar and vocabulary of the vernacular tongue of A. In 1716, Fr. M. de Lecce published a book entitled 'Osservazioni grammaticali Nella lingua Albanese.' And a dictionary of the Albanian language was published at Frankfort in 1835. The chief peculiarity of Albanian utterance is the predominance of nasal sounds. The name of Albanians seems to have been taken from Ptolemy by the Byzantine writers; for the Turks call them Arnauts, whilst their appellation in their own language is Skypetars; that of their country Skypiri, and their dialect Skyp. They are divided into four great tribes, or clans, called Dgedges, Toske or Toskides, Liapi or Liapides, and the Tsami, Chami, Tziamedes, or Ghegides. In each of these there are certain shades of difference with regard to language and manners, that render it possible for a native to distinguish them from each other. The first occupy the northern districts of Skutari, Dibre, Dulcigno, and Durazzo. The second dwell in the great plains of Albassan, Musakia, and Malacastra, stretching from the hills of Durazzo to Berat and Avlona, and along the banks of the Voyussa, or Aous, to Tepelini and Klissura, and from thence to the boundaries of Koritza eastward. The third are a race of poor predatory mountaineers, spread over the country between Toskeria and the great plain of Delvino, bounded by the Ionian sea. They are so dirty in their habits, and so addicted to plunder, that the derivation of their name is commonly referred to the Lapithæ of antiquity. The fourth tribe extends over that part of ancient Thesprotia which lies chiefly to the S of the river Kalamas or Thyamis, comprehending Philates, Margariti, and the Cassopœan mountains. " After the death of the great Scanderbeg, when the Albanians-who had made a most brave resistance-fell beneath the Ottoman yoke, an innovation was introduced into their religious faith. Till this time they had at least professed Christianity, however uninstructed they might have been in its peculiar tenets and doctrines. The progress of apostacy, how- | when he effected his settlement of Greece, attempted to give ever, was at first very slow, and the religion of Mahomet did not gain many adherents till about the end of the 16th century, when a law was promulgated which secured their estates in the possession of those Albanian families who should bring up one of their members in the Mahommedan faith. This had the double effect of keeping the country more clear of Osmanli settlers than the rest of Turkey, and of soon transferring the chief property into the hands of the new proselytes. At various times, however, whole villages, towns, or districts, for political advantages, have voluntarily renounced the religion of their ancestors; and such instances have occurred not unfrequently in the present times. Yet the Albanian Mahommedan is not more observant of doctrines, rites, and ceremonies, under his new law than he was under his old one, and is looked upon with great contempt by the rigid Osmanli, with whom the words Infidel and Albanian are nearly synonymous terms. He frequently takes a Christian woman to his wife, carries his sons to mosque, and allows his daughters to attend their mother to church; nay, he even goes himself alternately to both places of worship, and eats with his family out of the same dish, in which are viands forbidden to the disciples of Mahomet. Very few of them undergo the rite of circumcision. Hence when the pasha, in fits of religious zeal, sent sheiks to perform the operation throughout certain districts, many of the adults died in consequence. They are in general too poor to avail themselves of the license which their religion grants for polygamy, but are content with one wife, who is chosen, like any other animal, more for a slave or drudge, than for a companion. They are by no means jealous of their women, nor do they confine them like the Turks and Greeks. The wretched creature of a wife with one or two infants tied in a bag behind her back, cultivates the ground and attends to the household-affairs by turns, whilst her lordly master ranges over the forest in search of game, guards the flocks, or watches behind a rock with his fusil ready to aim at the unwary traveller. These women are in general tall and strong, but hardfeatured, with complexions rendered coarse by exposure to all varieties of weather." [Hughes.] Their common dress is of coarse cotton, with the head covered by a shawl which is clasped under their ears. Some of the younger women wear a kind of skull-cap, under which the hair is braided, and flows down strung with silver coins. One fault of very ill savour attaches itself to both sexes,—they are dirty in their habits to a proverb, and never lay aside their apparel either by day or night. Yet Mr. Hobhouse says, "the whole Albanian costume, when quite clean and new, is incomparably more elegant than any other worn in the Tuckish empire, and it may be made very costly." Their girdle usually consists of a coarse shawl, drawn very tight by a belt which contains their pistols. Their huts or cabins have no chimney, but plenty of crevices are left in the roof and walls for the egress of smoke and the admission of rain; nor is any other furniture often seen than a little earthenware, with an iron pot to cook their victuals. They are far from being luxurious in their food; and intoxication is a vice of rare occurrence. Their principal food is wheat or barley bread, or cakes of roasted maize, goats' milk cheese, rice, eggs, dried fish, olives, and vegetables; their proportion of animal food is small. "When an Albanian is completely armed, he carries a long musket over his shoulder, two pistols and an ataghan in his belt, with a narrow crooked sabre slung at his side, in a manner somewhat similar to our hussars. Thus equipped, and shorn after the fashion of the Abantes, with his little red skull-cap on his head,—his fleecy capote thrown carelessly over his shoulder,—his embroidered jacket,—his white camise, or kilt, and his scarlet buskins embossed with silver,—he calls himself a palikar, looks with infinite disdain upon the rest of the world, and in his gait assumes that haughty characteristic strut which is observable-though in a less degree - among our own Caledonian Highlanders." [Hughes.] A martial spirit and an eager thirst for gain lead the Albanian to engage very readily in the service of foreign states; and though an Albanian military force is rather a tumultuous assemblage of armed men than an army, yet they are always regarded as the most valuable of the Turkish troops. As a musical performer, the Albanian is not greatly in advance of his Greek neighbour, whose singing reminded Dr. E. D. Clarke "rather of the howling of dogs in the night than any sound which might be called musical." But whilst the ballads of the Greeks are generally love-ditties, those of the Albanians are "war-songs celebrating fierce and bloody encounters, deeds of plunder, and desperate achievements." Mr. Hobhouse says, that the A. "are distinguished even in a band of barbarians, for the singular cruelty and implacability of their disposition." The retribution of blood was in full force amongst them until the despotism of Ali Pasha put a stop to this evil, as well as to that system of brigandage which made robbery a profession, and removed from it every token of disgrace. History.] In the earliest ages of history we find the fierce tribes of Epirus, and to the N of these the still more savage Illyrians, in possession of the country now called Albania. The efforts of the Greeks, and of their successors the Romans, were alike unsuccessful in the attempt to civilize these barbarous tribes, who, retiring to their mountain-fastnesses, there retained their manners, and those remnants of a distinct language which probably formed the basis of the modern Albanian. When Augustus separated the provinces of the empire into Imperial and Prætorian, Macedonia and Illyria with Epirus, conjointly with Acarnania, Etolia, Thessaly, and the rest of Græcia Propria, formed the prov. of Achaia. Flaminius, them civil institutions, but speedily abandoned the project as impracticable, and satisfied himself with occupying the passes to their country. Under the reign of Constantine the Great, Illyricum and Epirus were comprised in Pannonia. On the decay of the empire, the Goths, under Alaric, ravaged Illyricum; and in the 6th cent., swarms of Bulgarians, issuing from the vast plains of Russia, Lithuania, and Poland, spread themselves over Epirus and Illyricum, and, by the end of the 10th cent., had got possession of the most southern parts of Epirus. "It was in these ages of Bulgarian prowess," says Major Leake, "that the remains of the Illyrian and Epirotic nations became finally included within the boundaries which they have ever since held. Many Sclavonian words thus found their way into the Albanian language, and have been increased in number by the intercourse between A. and the extensive regions of Servia and Bulgaria, which surround it on the N and E, and throughout which the Bulgarian dialect of Sclavonia is spoken." At the dismemberment of the Oriental empire, Michael Angelus, an illegitimate branch of the imperial family, established a despotate in this district embracing Ætolia, Acarnania, and Epirus, which maintained its independence until in 1478 it fell under the Turkish yoke, their subjugation having been warded-off for upwards of fifty years by the talents and heroism of Scanderbeg and his family. In the following age, they followed another chief of their own nation, the warlike Abraham, in his rapid career of conquest over Syria, Mesopotamia, and Arabia. Until the middle of last cent., A. was divided into several independent pashaliks. Ali Pasha, born at Topelini in 1751, having married the daughter of the pasha of Berat, overthrew the pasha of Janina or Joanina, and thereafter seized upon the pashaliks of Arta, Berat, Avlona, and Delvino in succession; and established himself almost as an independent sovereign over all those territories, and a wide extent of country beyond them, including nearly the whole of Thessaly and great part of Livadia. The line of coast extending southward from Durazzo, along the shores of the Adriatic, and the gulf of Corinth, formed the W and S boundaries of Ali Pasha's dominions; while the E was formed by the coast of the archipelago as far as a line drawn from Thermopylæ to the gulf of Corinth. This territory, according to the classical divisions of antiquity, comprehended the whole of Epirus and the southern part of Illyricum. His overthrow might have been followed by a union of the Albanians and Greeks in the war of freedom; but the overtures of the former were rejected by the Greeks who still remembered the enormous cruelties which had been perpetrated upon themselves and the Suliotes by Ali Pasha and his Albanian forces; and the Albanians were thus thrown into the arms of the Porte, to which they have since continued attached. Authorities. Clarke's Travels, vols. v. and vi.— Hobhouse's Albania, 2 vols. 4to., 1813.—Leake's Researches in Greece, 1814.—Holland's Travels, 2 vols. 8vo., 1815.—Vaudoncourt's Memoirs of the Ionian Islands, 8vo., 1816.—Bulletin de la Societe de Geographie, vol. xv.—Geographical Journal, vol. xii.—Istoria di Giorgio Castriolo detto Scanderberg da G. M. Biemme. Brescia 1742.—Pocqueville, Voyage dans la Grece. Paris 1821. 5 vols. 8vo.

ALBANIA, a name formerly given to that region of Asia which is bounded on the N by the Caucasian mountains; on the E by the Caspian; on the S by Armenia; and on the W by Iberia; and which now bears the name of Eastern Georgia or Shirwan.
ALBANKEUI, a town of Turkey, in the pashalik of Monastir, on a branch of the Potova, 9 m. NNW of Cailari.

ALBANO, a town of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, near a lake of the same name, 14 m. SSE of Rome, on the high road to Naples. It consists chiefly of one long street; but the beauty of the surrounding scenery, and the salubrity of the air, acquired by its elevation above sea-level of nearly 900 ft., have rendered it, in modern as in ancient times, the favourite resort of the patricians of Rome, whose villas and palaces give it an air of magnificence. It has been the seat of a bishopric since A. D. 460; and has a cathedral and some convents. Its population is estimated at 5,600.—The lake of A., a little to the NE of the town, is "one of the most beautiful pieces of water in the world; and, in respect to scenery, beyond comparison the finest of those of purely volcanic origin in Italy." [Gell.] It is of an elliptical form, surrounded with a high shelving shore which is covered with gardens and orchards. It is about <math>2\frac{1}{3}</math> m. in length, 13 m. in width, and more than 6 m. in circuit. Its surface is 918 ft. above sea-level; its water is ALB 1,000 ft. There can be little doubt that it has been formed in the crater of an extinct volcano. A current of lava traced nearly to Rome, as well as the volcanic tufa which alternates with other sediments below the soil, sufficiently prove the former activity of volcanic forces in this vicinity. [Phillips.] An 'emissario,' or subterranean canal, designed to protect the plain below from any sudden overflowing of the lake, was executed by the Romans, B. C., 394, and is still entire and perfect. It is 11,000 palms or 7,975 ft. in length, and varies in height from 7 to 10 ft. by 4 ft. in width; and is excavated or pierced chiefly through tufa rock. On the banks of the lake is Castel Gundolfo, the country-residence of the Pope, to which a fine road, shaded with double rows of ilex, leads from A. The same alley continues to "Grotta Ferrata, once the favourite villa of Cicero, and now an abbey of Greek monks." [Eustace.]—The summit of Mont' Albano, or Monte Cavo, rises 2,046 ft. above the surface of the lake, forming the most striking feature in the horizon around Rome. The view from the summit extends on the E to the Sabine ridge; on the N to Mount Soracte; and over "the whole of that classic ground which Virgil has made the scene of the last six books of the Æneid."—Gell's Topography of Rome.—Eustace's Classical Tour.—Piranesi Descrizione del Emissario del lago d'Albano.
ALBANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, 15 m. E by S of Potenza. Pop. 2,400.—Also a v. of Sardinia, 9 m. NW of Vercelli.—Also a town of Lombardy, in the Milanese, 6 m. E of Bergamo.
ALBANO (SANT'), a v. of Sardinia, in Savoy, on the Aisse river, 2 m. N of Chambery. Pop. 1,250.

ALBANS (SAINT), a town and parliamentary borough of Hertfordshire, finely situated on the summit and northern declivity of a hill rising on the banks of the Ver, a tributary of the Coln, 20 m. NW by W of London; 123 m. W by S of Hertford; and 63 NE of Watford station on the London and Birmingham railway. Pop. of the old borough in 1801, 3,038; in 1831, 4,772. Pop. of the extended parl. burgh in 1831, 5,771; in 1841, 6,497. It returns two members; electors in 1846, 412. The principal manufacture of the town is straw-plait for bonnets and hats. The abbey or conventual church, supposed to have been founded in 793, taken as an entire structure, is one of the most ancient and most magnificent ecclesiastical buildings in England. Though but a fragment of the once magnificent abbey of St. A., this building "attests the grandeur of the whole, and the perfection of ecclesiastical architecture in England during the middle ages. There is still extant, in the interior, specimens of genuine Saxon architecture, a part of the original building, the rounded arch, the massy tower, and enormous pillar, whose rude but noble simplicity is forcibly contrasted to the elaborated elegance of the Gothic style. Screens of the most minute tabernacle-work, pointed arches, feathery shafts, and a profusion of richly-sculptured tracery, display all the characteristic beauty of that most picturesque and fanciful epoch of the art. The high altar, the after-part of the choir, the chapel of Abbot Rambridge, and that of St. Alban, are the most remarkable. There are also existing beneath the fretted roof of this beautiful abbey church, monuments and tombs well-suited to revive remote associations with great events, and to awaken a poetic nationality in the most phlegmatic temperament. Of these, the tomb of the Protector, Duke of Gloucester, familiarly called the good Duke Humphry, stands on the southern side." [Lady Morgan.] The church is in the form of a cross; its total length from E to W, including the Lady-chapel, now used as a school, is 600 ft.; and its extreme breadth at the intersection of the transepts 200 ft.—St. "clear as crystal," and its depth is said to exceed | A. was the ancient Verulanium of the Romans; and the Watlingceaster of the Saxons. It derives its present name from Alban, an eminent citizen who suffered martyrdom here during the Dioclesian persecution, and to whose memory Offa, king of Mercia, founded a monastery here. The church of St. Michael in this town contains the ashes of the great Bacon. The family of Beauclerc take the title of duke from this town; and that of Grimston the title of earl from its ancient designation of Verulam.
ALBAN'S HEAD (SAINT), a promontory on the coast of Dorsetshire, 141 m. SE by E from Weymouth harbour; in N lat. 50° 4', W long. 2° 10'. The summit of the cliff is 441 ft. above sea-mark.

ALBANY, a co. of New York, U.S., on the W side of the Hudson, 150 m. N of New York. Area 515 sq. m. Pop. in 1830, 53,560; in 1840, 65,593; in 1845, 77,268, of whom 15,878 were voters, and 7,258 aliens not naturalized. Its surface is uneven; and in the SW part rises into hills. Along the Hudson, the soil is good, but in the interior it is sandy and unproductive. Besides the Mohawk on the N, and the Hudson on the E, it is watered by the Normanskill, Vlamanskill, Haivankranskill, and Coeyman's creek, all flowing to the Hudson. The Erie and Champlain canals form a junction at Watervliet, 8 m. N of Albany. The chief town is Albany, which is also the capital of the state. The aggregate valuation of real and personal estate in this co., in 1845, was 15,608,161 dollars; the total taxation 177,973 d. In 1840, there were 5 academies in this co. with 469 students; and 119 schools with 9,319 scholars.

ALBANY, the cap. of the above co., and the seat of government of the state of New York, and, in point of wealth, population, trade and resources, the second city in the state, is situated on the W bank of the Hudson, near the head of the tide-water, 145 m. by the course of the river above the city of New York; 164 m. W by N from Boston; 230 m. S of Montreal; in N lat. 42° 39′ 3″, W long. 73° 44′ 49″. [American Almanac.] Its pop. in 1810 was 9,356; in 1820, 12,630; in 1840, 33,721. The first appearance of A. is not prepossessing to a stranger: the older parts of the city were not laid out with much regularity, and often present gables turned to the street; but the more recent streets are spacious and regular. The capitol, at the head of State-street which rises in a straight course from the river to the brow of a hill 220 ft. in alt., is a fine edifice. The city-hall is also a handsome edifice; and in 1844, A. contained 11 public squares, 3 markets, and 30 churches. A. is chiefly interesting as a place of trade and commerce. Situated on one of the finest rivers in the U.S., and having a rich back-country, its natural advantages both as a place of trade and transit are great; and these have been vastly increased by the formation of the Erie and the Champlain canals. The Erie canal, in union with the Champlain, enters in one channel the N part of A., and opens into a spacious basin. By the Erie canal and a line of railroads A. is connected with Buffalo, the great lakes, and the whole trade of the W; by the Champlain canal, with the Hudson, the St. Lawrence, and all the country N. A railroad extends from A. to Saratoga, 38 m.; and a railway running along the E bank of the Hudson to New York, which will place the two towns within 4 or 5 hours of each other, is now [1849] nearly completed. The tidal-flow of the Hudson reaches 5 m. above A.; and vessels of 80 tons ascend as far as A. In 1840, 20 steamboats, and 21 tow-boats plied between A. and New York. The voyage is usually made in 12 hours. The manufactures of A. are carriages, hats, tobacco, leather, ropes, candles, musical instruments, tin and iron ware, printing-types, oil-cloth, carpets, &c. In 1840, the total cap. employed in manufactures was 1,735,500 dollars.—A. | beyond Lattaku on another. Meanwhile the rearing ALB was founded by the Dutch in 1623, and by them called Beaver Wyck, and afterwards Williamstadt. It capitulated to the English, who gave it its present name, in 1664, from James II., then Duke of York and Albany, to whom Charles II. granted the colony. It was incorporated in 1686.

ALBANY, is likewise the name of various minor towns in the United States: viz. In Oxford co. Maine, 18 NW of Paris. Pop. 691.—In Strafford co. New Hampshire, 6 m. N by E of Concord. Pop. 406.—In Orleans co., 39 m. N of Montpelier. Pop. 920.—In Berks co. Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,057.—There are v. of the name in Bradford co. Pennsylvania; in Clinton co. Kentucky; in Henry co. Tennessee; in Whiterider co. Illinois; and in Baker co. Georgia.

ALBANY, a district of Southern Africa on the coast to the W of Algoa bay; bounded on the N by Somerset territory and the Kunap or northern branch of the Great Fish river; on the E by the Great Fish river which separates it in this quarter from the Neutral Ground now ceded territory; on the S by the Atlantic; and on the W by the Bushman's or Sunday river which separates it from Uitenhage district. It lies between the meridians of 26° and 27° E; and its extreme N point, near the junction of the Kaga and Kunap, is under the parallel of 32° 40' S. The superficies is estimated at 1,792 sq. m.; or 1,200,000 acres. The Kaffir Berg range of mountains intersects its northern districts, dividing the streams which flow N and E to the Kunap, from those which descend S to the Great Fish river above its junction with the Kunap. The Fish River Berg runs nearly through the centre of the country from WNW to ESE. Further to the S, on the W confines of the district, is the Zwartwater Berg; and to the S of it, the Assegai Berg. The principal river is the Great Fish river. Between the mouth of this river and that of Bushman's river, the principal streams falling into the Atlantic are the Lynedoch, the Kowie, and the Kareega. The mouths of all these rivers are much obstructed by sand-bars. The climate of A. is "one of the healthiest in the world." [Alexander.] The surface is varied with mountains of considerable elevation, grassy hills, wooded cloofs or ravines, rich pasture plains, and streams and rills. Unfortunately, however, there is a want of water throughout the district: for as the land increases rapidly in elevation towards the interior, the streams have gradually, in the course of ages, worn themselves deep channels, much below the surface of the country, with very precipitous banks.—The cereal grains and pulse of Europe, as well as most culinary vegetables, succeed well on the plains; and horses, cattle, sheep, hogs, and goats, thrive remarkably well. Indurated limestone is quarried near Bathurst; and some indications of iron have been met with. An extensive bed of lead-ore has recently been discovered in the original formation near Algoa bay. The ore is said to contain nearly 60 per cent. of lead, and a not inconsiderable proportion of silica.—The capital is Graham's Town, which is situated nearly in the centre of the prov., 35 m. from the sea, on the sources of the Kowie. The other towns are Bathurst, Theopolis, Salem, and Port Francis at the mouth of the Kowie. The district of A. was originally colonized in 1820 by about 4,000 British subjects who, landing at Port Elizabeth, made their way to Graham's Town—then merely a military post of 22 houses and huts-whence they scattered themselves in small parties over the country, each male settler receiving 100 acres. In eighteen months they had created a profitable trade with the Kaffirswho exchanged hides, horns, and ivory, for beads, buttons, and brasswire-and had penetrated with their waggons to Natal on the one side, and even of cattle, horses, and fine-wooled sheep, went on prosperously for ten years, and the pop. rose to 11,000. Graham's Town increased to 3,000; and Port Elizabeth, on Algoa bay-which may be considered the port of this district-from 4 houses in 1820, had, in 1834, a pop. of 1,200; while the exports amounted to £89,888; and the imports to £131,697. This state of prosperity was blighted by Kaffir invasion, and a long series of deplorable commandoes on the one side, and forays on the other. British Kaffirland having now had its E frontier advanced to the White Kei river, and the Gaika Kaffirs having given in their submission, it may be hoped that A. will speedily regain all its former prosperity, and make rapid advances towards the comfort and security of a fully settled and organized territory.

ALBANY, a large river of British N. America, which rises in Lake St. Joseph, in N lat. 51° 30', W long. 90° 30', and flowing E discharges itself by a large estuary into James's bay, after a course of about 340 m. Its navigation is interrupted by several rapids and falls, between which and the coast the bed of the river is composed of limestones and clays. The Hudson's Bay company have four trading-stations on this river; viz., at Osnaburg near its issue from Lake St. Joseph; Gloucester about 130 m. below it; Henley at the fork formed by the junction of the S branch with the main stream; and Albany fort on an island below the Great fall at its embouchure, in N lat. 52° 18', W long. 81° 45'.—Also a district of Western Australia, lying along King George's sound, 60 m. to the E of Nornaleep inlet.
ALBARESE, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. of Sienna, 7 m. S by E of Grossetto.

ALBARRACIN, or ALBARAZZIN, a fortified town of Spain, in Aragon, on the l. bank of the Guadalaviar, 18 m. WNW of Teruel, at the foot of the Sierra d'Albarracin. It is an ancient town, and the seat of a bishop, a suffragan of Saragossa. Pop. 2,200. The wool of the small sheep fed on the partido d'Albarracin is considered the finest grown in Spain.
ALBAS, a town of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. and 12 m. W by N of Cahors. Pop. 1,800.
ALBASANO. See ELBASSAN.

ALBASYNE, a town and fort on the Amur river, in the Mantshu territory, 700 versts distant from Nertchinsk, founded by an adventurous party of Russian Cossacks, who were eventually dispossessed of it by the jealousy of the Chinese. The soil of the surrounding district is said to be fertile, and the climate comparatively temperate, a chain of very high mountains affording shelter from the N winds.
ALBATERA, a town of Spain, 27 m. NE of Murcia. Pop. 2,400. There is a magnificent church here. Silk worms are extensively reared in the environs.

ALBATROSS ISLAND, the northernmost of Hunter's isles, at the entrance of Bass's straits; in S lat. 40° 25′, E long. 144° 40½′. [Flinders.] It is 2 m. in length, and sufficiently high to be seen at 5 leagues distance. Its shores are steep, and the cliffs appear almost white with birds. Capt. Flinders says, that when his explorers first landed on this island they were obliged to fight their way up the cliffs against the seals; and, when they arrived at the top, to make a road with their clubs amongst the albatrosses.
ALBATROSS POINT, the N extremity of a pen- North island of New Zealand, forming the W side of Kawia harbour.

ALBAY, a town and district on the coast of Lucon, at the foot of the volcanic mountain of Albay, or Mahon, by which it has often been devastated, especially in 1814. The pop. of the prov. of A., which forms the S part of the peninsula of Camarines, in 1837, was 131,745. The interior of this district is ALB inhabited by a Malay tribe whom the Spaniards call Ygorottes.
ALBAYDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 6 m. S of Jativa. Pop. 3,200.—Also a town of Spain, in Old Castile, 6 m. S of Logrono, near the Yregua river.
ALBBRUGG, a v. of Baden, in the bailiwick of Waldshut, at the confluence of the Alb with the Rhine. Iron is manufactured here into various light and fancy articles.
ALBE, a lake of France, in the dep. of Hautes- Pyrénées, on the N side of the Maladetta, at an alt. of 8,445 ft. above sea-level.—De Charpentier.

ALBECK, a bailiwick of Wurtemberg, in the cirele of the Danube, watered by the Lonthal. Pop. in 1817, 13,953. Its principal town, of the same name, is 6 m. NNE of Ulm. Pop. 407. The Austrian general Mack was defeated in the neighbourhood of A. by the French in October 1805.

ALBEGNA, or Albenda, a river of Tuscany, which, rising 9 m. N of Saturnia in the Monte Labro, and running from NE to SW, flows into the Mediterranean, a little to the N of Lake Orbitello, after a course of 40 m.

ALBEMARLE, a central co. of Virginia, U. S. It has the Blue ridge on the NW, and James' river on the S; and is intersected by the head-waters of the Rivanna. Its area is 700 sq. m. Its principal production is tobacco. Pop. in 1840, 22,924, of whom 13,809 were slaves. Its cap. is Charlottesville.—Also the largest of the Galapago islands. It is about 70 m. in length, and stretches N and S. Its eastern coast is nearly straight; but its western side is deeply concave, and embraces the active volcanic island of Narborough. The N. head of A. terminates westwardly in Cape Berkley, which, according to the officers of the Potomac, is exactly on the equatorial line; but Captain Pipon places one of the Rodondo rocks off the N end of the island in 22' N, and Colnette's chart in 15' N. Cape Woodford, which projects from the S head of the island, is from 18 to 20 m. NW of Charles's island or Floriana. The channel between A. and Narborough islands in one part is not more than 3 m. wide. At its narrowest part is a very narrow entrance into a snug cove in A. island, not more than three cables length wide, and not quite a mile in length, but in which six frigates might ride with safety. Fish is plentiful everywhere, and the beaches are filled with turtle.

ALBEMARLE SOUND, a large inlet on the NE coast of N Carolina, U. S., extending 60 m. from E to W, and from 4 to 15 m. wide. It communicates with Pimlico sound and the ocean by several narrow inlets, and with Chesapeake bay by a canal through the Dismal swamp. At its western extremity, it receives the Chowan and Roanoke rivers, of which it may be regarded as the continuation or common estuary.

ALBEN, a town of Austria, in Carniola, on a mountain of the same name, 2 m. NW of Zirknitz, in which quicksilver is wrought.—Also a small stream of Bavaria, issuing from Köning's lake and flowing into the Saltza, 6 m. above Saltzburg.

ALBENDORF, a town of Prussian Silesia, in the circle of Breslau, 9 m. NW of Glatz. Pop. 1,000. There is a celebrated place of pilgrimage in the neighinsular piece of wooded land on the W coast of the | bourhood of A. called New Jerusalem, which is annually visited by from 60,000 to 80,000 Catholics.

ALBENGA, an administrative division of Sardinia, on the Genoese coast, comprising 53 communes, and a pop. in 1821 of 52,594. Its cap. of the same name is a handsome town, 21 m. NNE from Point Coscione, near the mouth of the Centa, 44 m. SW of Genoa. Pop. 5,000. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of the abp. of Genoa. Though situated in CONTRACTOR OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF THE STATE OF a beautiful and well-cultivated plain, the town is considered unhealthy. It is the Alba Inguanorum or Albium Inquanum of antiquity; and was the birth-place of Proculus, who competed with Probus for the throne of the Cæsars.—Half-a league S from A., and about a mile from the shore, is a small islet of the same name surmounted by a watch-tower. It is sometimes called Galinara.
ALBENREUTH (OLD and New), two v. of Bohemia, 9 m. SSE of Elnbogen, and within a mile of each other, near to which are mines of magnesia and cobalt, and mineral springs.

ALBENS, a v. of Italy, in Savoy, 9 m. NNE of Aix. Pop. 1,300. Coins of the later Roman emperors are frequently discovered in the neighbourhood of this place.—Also a river of Bavaria. See ABENS. ALBERCHE—pronounced Alverche—a river of Spain, which rises in the Navacabeza mountain, 5 m. S of Villafranca in Avila, runs from NW to SE as far as Aldea del Fresno, where it turns SW, and entering the prov. of Toledo, passes Escalona and flows into the Tagus near Talavera de la Reyna, after a course of 100 m.

ALBE'RES MOUNTAINS, the eastern portion of the Pyrenean chain, which separates the French dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales from Spain. It rises abruptly from the Mediterranean in the rugged Cape de Creux, the easternmost point of Spain, in N lat. 42° 194', E long. 3° 16'; and soon attains an elevation of from 1,600 to 2,000 ft. At about 28 m. to the E of the Col de Pertus, it branches towards the NW, and joins the main chain at the head of the valley of Tech, in the Pic of the Canigou [alt. 9,141 ft., Vidal], in N lat. 42° 31', E long. 2° 25'. Here the gently swelling hills of the A. give place to sharp craggy peaks and precipitous masses of rock. The great pass from France into Spain, by the route of Perpignan, crosses the chain at the Col de Pertus.
ALBERG, or AALBERGER, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, 5 m. E by N of Almeloo. Pop. 900.

ALBERGARIA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, 15 m. N of Beia. - Also a town in the prov. of Beira, 15 m. NE by N of Aveiro.—The former is sometimes known as A. Dos Fuzos; the latter as A. Nova.
ALBERIQUE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. SSW of Valencia. Pop. 2,000.
ALBERNAS (PONTA), the NW point of the island of Flores, one of the Azores. It is moderately high, sloped, and of a red colour.
ALBERONA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, cant. of Biccari, 6 m. SE of Volturara. Pop. 2,500.
ALBERRI (CANAL OF), an islet on the SW coast of Vancouver's island, in N lat. 49°, W long. 124° 35'.
ALBERSCHWEND, a town of Austrian Tyrol, in the Vorarlberg, 6 m. SSE of Bregentz. Pop. 1,000.
ALBERSDORF, a town of Denmark, in Holstein, in Suderditmarsch.

ALBERT, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of the Somme, arrond. of Péronne.—The cant. comprises 26 communes, and had a pop. of 16,056 in 1831.—The com. and v. are 18 m. ENE of Amiens, on the Miraumont stream, and intersected by the railroad from Paris to Boulogne. Pop. 2,828. Its ancient name was Ancre, which it changed on the condemnation of Concini, Marshal D'Ancre.

ALBERT, a fine river of N Australia, flowing through a rich alluvial country into the head of the great gulf of Carpentaria. It was discovered by the officers of the Beagle exploring expedition, and followed up in boats to 50 m. from the entrance.

ALBERT (LAKE), a branch or inlet from Lake Victoria in South Australia, about 50 m. SE by S I. ALB from Adelaide. Its S extremity is separated by a very narrow neck of land from the Coorong, a long shallow narrow lagune which follows the outline of the coast of Encounter bay.
ALBERTACCE, a com. of Corsica, in the cant. of Calacuccia. Pop. 1,020.
ALBERTI, a town of Hungary, 27 m. SE of Pest, on the line of the proposed canal from the Theiss to the Danube.
ALBESTI, a town of Upper Wallachia, 85 m. NNW of Bucharest.

ALBESTROFF, a cant., v., and com. of France, in the dep. of La Meurthe, near the canal of Salines, 18 m. ESE of Château-Salins. Pop. of cant., comprising 26 communes, in 1831, 12,829; of com. and v. 842.
ALBEUVE, a com. and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Friburg, on the Saine river, 6 m. SE of Gruyère. Pop. 485.
ALBI, a com. and town of Savoy, 9 m. SW of Annecy. Pop. 1,200. See also Alba and Alby.
ALBIAS, a com. of France, in the dep. of Tarnet-Garonne, cant. of Negrepelisse. Pop. 1,257.
ALBIDONA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, 12 m. NE of Cassano, near the head of a stream flowing into the gulf of Tarento. Pop. 1,100.

ALBIGEOIS, an ancient district of France, in the N of Upper Languedoc, now in the dep. of Tarn, in which it forms the arrond. of Alby, and the greater part of that of Gaillac. In 992 this district belonged to the counts of Toulouse; in the 12th cent. it was held by the counts of Carcassone. It gave the name Albigenses to the early reformers of France whose opinions were condemned in a council held at Alby in 1176. After the termination of the crusade against these heroic men, the territory of the Albigeois was bestowed upon its conqueror Simon de Montfort, whose son ceded his rights to Louis VIII.
ALBIGOWA, a town of Austrian Galicia, 15 m. E of Rzeszow. Pop. 1,000.

ALBINEN, a v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, 13 m. N of the baths of Leuk, perched on the top of the precipice that hems in the valley of Leuk on every side like a huge wall, and at least 900 ft. above the level of the valley. The only direct mode of communication between the inhabitants of Leuk and this v. is by a series of nearly a dozen rude ladders going up the face of the precipice, and fastened to the rock with hooked sticks.

ALBINO, a town of Lombardy, in the Milanese, on the r. bank of the Serio, 7 m. NNE of Bergamo. Pop. 2,300. A good deal of raw silk is thrown here; and the whet-stones which are quarried in the neighbourhood and polished here are celebrated over Europe.—There is a cape of this name on the coast of Benguela to the S of Cape Negro, formed by some remarkable steep white cliffs.

ALBINO, a Portuguese word applied to individuals of the human race who have the skin and hair white; the iris very pale, bordering on red; and the eye so sensible that they cannot bear the light of day. This condition is seen more frequently in the Negro. The Albino-called Blafard on the continent of Europe; Bedas, Chacrelas, or Kakerlaks, in India; White-Negro or Dondos, in Africa; Darien in America-is an individual malformation or degeneration in the colouring matter of the skin and hair, usually dying with the individual; but it sometimes becomes hereditary, and is transmitted to their offspring. It presents the same characters in whatever race it appears, and is found likewise among the lower animals. The human Albinos are of a feeble constitution; their skin of a dull white, the eyes weak with the iris red, and the hair of a pale yellow. They are most commonly found-or at least are most remarked - among the races of dark complexions. At Java, they are reported to form a wandering and proscribed race, roaming in the woods under the name of Chacrelas. Labillardière observed an Albino female of Malay descent upon one of the Friendly islands. The Albinos of Ceylon, called Bedas or Bedos, appear to belong to the Hindu race. They are also found among the Papus; and have been seen, but very seldom, among the Hyperboreans. Ptolemy and Pliny apply the term Leucathiopes to a tribe of people in Nigritia. A white Negress from Madagascar was observed by M. Bory de St. Vincent. She had two children, the one by a white, the other by a Negro: each of these children presented intermediate characters between its parents, having the usual traits of the father combined with the Albino features and white hair of the mother. Albinos are reported to be common in the woods of the Isle of France. They are also common on the continent of Africa. In America, the most remarkable are the Dariens, who reside in the isthmus connecting the northern and southern portions of that continent. The want of colour, or albinism, both in animal and vegetable bodies, has its proximate cause in the original want, or the diminished secretion, of the rete mucosum, or coloured layer of mucous net-work placed immediately under the epidermis or outer skin of animals. With plants, this is owing to the inert secretion of the green matter, or chromule, and its ceasing to colour the cuticular tissues. In all species, softness and moisture are the results of this albinism or whiteness. Its ultimate cause is the want of vital energy, arising either from the prolonged absence of the influence of light upon the organic structure, or from the intensity of a long-continued cold. Its effects may be either absolute and total, or merely partial and local, even among the white varieties of animals and plants. Its general tendency is to effeminate all beings. Accidental albinism may arise from old age, or the want of a continued renewal of this coloured layer, which communicates its hue to the hair, feathers, or scales. It may be even induced before old age by disease, or by the absence of the usual supplies of nutriment, or, among animals, by the violence of fear or any sudden emotion, which may serve to withdraw from the exterior of the body its secretions, and render the skin pale, or the hair white. There is also an accidental albinism from the mechanical injury of the mucous pigment, arising from the bruising or tearing of the skin, and on these spots, white hair or feathers will arise in the place of coloured appendages. An opposite state of deep blackness, or melanism, when the surface is said to be melanose or black, arises from the superabundance of the mucous subcutaneous tissue in animals and plants, in which carbon exudes towards the exterior. Such are Negroes, and all black or dark-brown animals, and lurid and venomous plants, as the Solaneæ. This state of the skin is well-fitted for skies resplendent with light and heat. It is attended in individuals with dryness, rigidity, and shortness of stature. Excessive cold, combined with the absence of light, serves to drive the nutritive and repairing juices far from the skin. This kind of albinism is especially remarked in animals inhabiting the highest mountains and the polar regions, where they become white in winter and coloured in summer.—Cuvier. ALBION, ALBINN, or ALBANY, the ancient Gaelic name of Scotland, and, until Cæsar's time, the original appellation of the whole island of Great Britain. The Scottish Celts denominate themselves Gael Albinn or Albinnich, in distinction from those of Ireland, whom they call Gael Eirinnich; and the Irish themselves call the Scottish Gaels, Albannaich; while their writers, so late as the 12th cent., call the country of the Scottish Gael, Alban. With respect to the etymology of the name Albinn or Albion, it is to be observed, in the first place, that it is compounded of two syllables, the last of which, inn, signifies in Celtic a large island. Thus far the etymology is clear; but the meaning of the adjective part, alb, is not so apparent. Dr. John Macpherson thinks it folly to search for a Hebrew or Phoenician etymon of Albion; and he considers the prefix alb as denoting a high country, the word being, in his opinion, synonymous with the Celtic vocable alp or alba, which signifies high.
