"Of the Alpes Grajæ, Alpes Pæninæ or Penninæ, and the Alpes Bastarnicæ, every man of letters," says he, "has read. In the ancient language of Scotland, alp signifies invariably an eminence. The Albani, near the Caspian sea, the Albani of Macedon, the Albani of Italy, and the Albanich of Britain, had all the same right to a name founded on the same characteristical reason,—the height or roughness of their respective countries. The same thing may be said of the Gaulish Albici, near Massilia." Deriving alb from the Latin word albus, the appellation of Albinn would denote an island distinguished by some peculiarity either in the whiteness of its appearance or in the productions of its soil; and hence Pliny derives the etymon of Albion from its white rocks washed by the sea, or from the abundance of white roses which the island produced. His words are, " Albion insula sic dicta ab albis rupibus, quas mare alluit, vel ob rosas albas quibus abundat." But although the whitish appearance of the English cliffs, as seen from the channel and the opposite coast of Gaul, certainly appears to support the supposition of Pliny, yet it is evidently contrary to philological analogy to seek for the etymon of Albion in the Latin. Amongst the various opinions given on this subject, that of Dr. Macpherson seems to be the most rational.—The term Albany, or Alban, became ultimately the peculiar appellation of an extensive Highland district comprehending Breadalbane, Athole, part of Lochaber, Appin, and Glenorchy. The title Duke of Albany was first created for a younger son of Robert II. It became extinct in his son Murdoch, who was beheaded by James I. James II. renewed it for his second son Alexander; in whose son it again became extinct. Since the Union it has always been borne by the king's second son.

ALBION, a town in Kennebec co. in the state of Maine, U. S., 26 m. NE of Augusta. Pop. 1,624.— Also the cap. of Orleans co. in the state of New York, ~ ALB 250 m. W by N of Albany, on the Erie canal. Pop. 1,400.—Also a township in Oswego co. New York, 147 m. WNW of Albany. Pop. 1,503.—Also a township in Calhoun co. Michigan, on the Kalamazu. Pop. 932.—Also a township in Edwards co. Illinois, 40 m. SW of Vincennes.

ALBION (New), a designation first given by Sir Francis Drake to a portion of the NW coast of America, probably from some resemblance in the white cliffs near Punta de los Reyes to those of his own country. [Beechey.] The whole of this coast between the parallels of 38° and 48° has been denominated New Albion, on the supposition that it was first traced by Drake throughout this extent; but the coast of New California had been examined up to the parallel of 43° N by Cabrillo and Ferrelo, two Portuguese navigators, in 1542-3, or thirty-six years previous to Drake's voyage; and the term N. A. ought therefore to be restricted to that part of the coast which lies between the parallels of 43° and 48°, if indeed Fletcher, the narrator of 'The Famous Voyages of Sir Francis Drake,' is correct in his assertion that they coasted the land to the height of 48°, which has been sharply questioned by American writers. The geographical features of this region are noticed under the articles Oregon and California.

ALBIS, a mountain-chain of Switzerland, which, commencing in the canton of Zug, runs NNW along the l. bank of the Sihl in a course nearly parallel with the lake of Zurich; and then NW along the l. bank of the Limmat. The highest summit of the chain is the Hütliberg, a little to the SW of Zurich, which has an alt. of 2,790 ft. The northern road across the A. from Zurich to Lucerne passes under this summit from the v. of Albisrieden, 3 m. NW from Zurich, and descends upon Knonau; but another and more frequented road, after leading from Zurich along the lake to Adliswyl, ranges off over the Sihl, and ascends by a series of terraces of easy slope to the Albis Wirthshaus, whence it winds upwards, amid immense boulders and jutting crags of breccia, by a road not too steep for a char-à-bonc, to the summit of the pass, [alt. 2,404 ft.,] and then descends on the W side to Knonau, by the Turlersee or Durlesee, which it passes on the r. One of the finest and most comprehensive views in Switzerland is obtained from the Schabelberg or Hochwacht, which may be gained in half-an-hour from the summit of this pass. "To the E you command the whole lake and the greater part of the canton of Zurich, with the mountains of the Toggenberg, and the districts of Gaster and Uznach. To the S the Alps rise, with their sublimities of snow, pyramids, and glaciers, extending from the Sentis to the Jungfrau. To the N you overlook a vast extent of country, including most parts of Arrau, the northern section of Zurich, and part of Schaffhausen and Thurgau, until the prospect terminates at the conical summits of Hohentwiel and the curves of the Black Forest. On the W extend and arise the valleys and mountains of Zug, Lucerne, and Bern, the Rhigi and the Pilatus,—the Durlesee, and the lake of Zug." [Macgregor's Note-Book.]—In the campaign of 1799, the French under Massena, held the slope of the Albis, while the Russian forces lay on the r. bank of the Sihl, for above three months.
ALBISSOLA, or Albizzola, a v. of Sardinia, 5 m. NE of Savona, stretching along the coast of the Mediterranean. In the environs are several country villas of the Genoese nobility.
ALBOCACER, a town of Spain, in Valencia, 30 m. NNW of Castellon de la Plana.
ALBON, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Drôme, arrond. of Valence, 6 m. NNE of Saint-Vallier. Pop. 2,663.
ALBONA, a town of Illyria, near the mouth of the Arsa, in the gulf of Quarnaro, 22 m. E of Rovigno. Pop. 1,100.
ALBOR, a town of Portugal, on the coast of Algarve, 7 m. E of Lagos.—Also one of the Bahama group, between San Salvador and Nequa.

ALBORAN, an islet of the Mediterranean, off the coast of Spain, in N lat. 35° 56', W long. 3° 03'. [Rumker.] It is a sterile limestone rock, about halfa-mile in length from E to W, and one quarter in breadth, inhabited only by sea-birds, and with no other vegetation than the Rocella tinctoria, or Dyer's lichen. In the vicinity, the current in general sets strongly to the eastward. The present variation of the needle near this island is 22° W.
ALBOURN, a p. in Sussex, 2 m. NW from Hurst- Pierpoint. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. 395.
ALBOURS. See ELBURZ.
ALBOX, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Almeria, on a branch of the Almanzora, 42 m. NE of Almeria.
ALBRECHT (SAINT), a town of Prussia, in the circle and 3 m. S of Dantzic. Pop. 791.
ALBRECHTS, a town of Prussian Saxony, 10 m. NE of Meiningen. Pop. 710.
ALBRECHTSBERG, a v. of Austria, 9 m. W of St. Polten, between the Great and Little Krems. Pop. 170.

ALBREDA, a town and French factory of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Barra, 1 m. from the r. bank of the Gambia, and 25 m. from its embouchure, nearly opposite to James Fort. Bowdich says it is "a known emporium for slaves and smuggling," in spite of the British establishment at Bathurst. Pop. 700. ALBRET. or LALBRIT, a v. of France, in the dep. of Landes, and arrond. of Mont-de-Maison, which gave name to the county and duchy of Albret in Gascony. Pop. 1,000.
ALBRIGHTON, a p. and v. of Salop. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 1,058. The v. is 4 m. N of Shrewsbury.
ALBSHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Rhine, on the Ers river, 12 m. W of Worms.

ALBUERA, or ALBUHERA, a v. of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, 16 m. SE by S of Badajoz, on a rivulet of the same name which flows into the Guadiana. On the 16th of May, 1811, a sanguinary battle was fought here by the French under Soult, and an allied force of British, Spanish, and Portuguese troops under Beresford. The rivulet runs parallel to the front of the heights which formed the English position, and at about 600 yards' distance; and the v. of A. was nearly opposite the centre of the allied force, amounting to 30,000 infantry—of which, however, little more than 6,000 were British— 2,000 cavalry, and 38 guns. Soult, who commanded 19,000 infantry, 4,000 cavalry, and 50 guns, for a time had the advantage; but a charge of the British infantry, at a critical moment, decided the fortune of the day. The French lost 8,000, and the allies 7,000 men in this sanguinary conflict.
ALBUFEIRA, or Albofeira, a seaport of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, 37 m. W of Tavira. It is defended by a citadel and batteries. Pop. 3,000.

ALBUFERA, a large lagune, in the Spanish prov. of Valencia, 5 m. S of the city of Valencia. It is about 12 m. in length from N to S, and 6 in breadth, but so shallow as scarcely to float even small boats. It communicates with the sea by a narrow channel, which is closed with sluices. Rice is cultivated upon its shores; and fish and waterfowl are taken in abundance from it, and form a source of considerable annual revenue, which, being attached to the Crown, was given to the Duke of Wellington as a reward for his services. Napoleon created Suchet duke of A. for the capture of Valencia in 1812.—The name, which is derived I ALB from the Arabic article al, and boheira, the diminutive of bahr, 'a lake,'—is given to several lagunes on the coasts of Spain and Portugal; and is sometimes applied by the country-people even to artificial reservoirs such as those in the neighbourhood of Merida.

ALBULA, a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, to the NE of Mont Julier. A high and difficult pass from Bergun to the Upper Engadine leads across this mountain. The summit of this pass is 7,648 ft. above sea-level, or 100 ft. higher than that of Mount St. Bernard. From Bergun to the first interior valley there is a road mounting by the side of a torrent, and practicable for small carts; this leads into an Alpine valley of about 3 m. in length, at the extremity of which the path begins to ascend a narrow defile to the little lake of Wissenstein. From this lake the ascent is extremely rapid; and in about an hour enters a defile from 1 to 2 m. broad, and 3 or 4 m. in length, around which the highest summits of the mountain shoot up almost perpendicularly,-in some places showing precipices of 2,000 or 3,000 ft.,-in others presenting a forest of towers and pinnacles,-or displaying enormous gaps,—while above all the highest peaks, "powdered with snow, but too ragged and pointed to allow it a restingplace," jut into the sky. The whole extent of this defile is one mass of enormous stones piled upon each other, in some places to the height of some hundred feet, "as if two mountains of rock had here waged war and been shivered in the conflict." As we ascend, the head-stream of the Albula is seen to pour into this defile, but its course through it towards the lake of Wissenstein is only heard, not seen. In the third and last division of the pass, the scenery is somewhat softened, a scanty herbage appears, enlivened here and there by the ranunculus and mountain-anemone, and the rocks are covered with the crimson blossoms of the rhododendron. At the highest part of the pass, "one is still in a valley, though its sides do not rise more than 1,000 ft. above it." The descent into the Engadine is less by at least 2,000 ft. than the ascent from Bergun on the other side.—The A. river flows first SW and then NW, and after a course of about 40 m., flows into the Upper Rhine.

ALBUQUERQUE, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, on a hill rising from the banks of the Gevora, 25 m. NNW of Badajoz. It has an old and strong castle. Pop. 6,000.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, on the r. bank of the Paraguay.—Also a town of New Mexico, on the l. bank of the Rio Grande, 55 m. SW by S of Santa Fe. Pop. 6,000.
ALBUQUERQUE KAYS, three islets off the Mosquito shore, in the Caribbean sea; in N lat. 12° 93', W long. 81° 51'; 22 m. S by W from St. Andrew's.
ALBURG, a port in the NW corner of Vermont, U. S., on a triangular point of land projecting into Lake Champlain; 40 m. N of Burlington. Pop. 1,344.
ALBURGH, a p. in Norfolk, 4 m. E by N from Harleston. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 589.
ALBURNO (MONTE), a detached ridge of the Central Apennines, in the Neapolitan prov. of Principato Citra, extending about 20 m. from NW to SE, between the Tanagro and the Calore rivers.

ALBURY, a p. in Hertfordshire, 43 m. NW from Bishop-Stortford. Area 3,200 acres. Pop. 641.— Also a p. in Oxfordshire, 3 m. NW of Tetsworth. Area 1,250 acres. I'op. 244.—Also a p. in Surrey, 5 m. SE of Guilford. Area 4,920 acres. Pop. 1,079.
ALBUSSAC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, arrond. of Tulla. Pop. 1,336.

ALBUTY, a name given in Northern Siberia to flat valleys which, though dried up in summer, are occasionally filled with water in spring, when they form lakes of various sizes, which form one of the peculiar features of that country. The rich alluvial soil when thus cleared of water is speedily covered with a luxuriant growth of fine grass, and the Jakuti never fail to settle near these fresh pastures. Hence the frequent occurrence of the term albut or albuty in the topography of this district. - Wrangel.
ALBY, a p. in Norfolk, 5 m. N by E of Aylsham. Area 840 acres. Pop. 299.

ALBY, or ALBI, a town of France, the cap. of the dep. of Tarn, situated in the midst of a fertile plain intersected by the river Tarn; 35 m. SW of Rhodez, and 42 m. NE of Toulouse; in N lat. 43° 56', W long. of Paris 0° 12'. Alt. above sea-level 554 ft. Pop. in 1789, 8,684; in 1842, 12,408. It is of considerable antiquity; and was the Albiga or Albia of the Middle ages; but few monuments of its former greatness have survived the vicissitudes which have # four times consigned it to ruins and depopulation. The ramparts have been converted into public walks and gardens; and a fine promenade occupies the site of the ancient lists, and commands a fine view of the adjacent country. The streets are narrow and irregular in their outline; but the houses, which are of brick, are generally well-built. The principal edifices are the cathedral of St. Cecile, a Gothic structure of the 13th cent., possessing a beautiful choir; the Prefecture, formerly the episcopal palace, and originally the residence of the counts of the Albigeois; the church of St. Salvi, and the hospital of St. Jacques. A. has a museum of natural history, a library consisting of 12,000 vols., and a theatre. Its manufactures consist of woollen, linen, and cotton fabrics of various descriptions, moleskin, hats, files, scythes, sealing-wax, candles, woad-pastiles, and leather. In the environs are a copper work, some paper-mills, a china manufactory, and an iron foundery. The surrounding plain-which is one of the richest grain-districts in France-produces, besides corn, plums, grapes, saffron, and the dyers' plant called woad. Numerous flocks of sheep are reared in its pasturages. Coals are wrought in considerable quantity at Carmeaux, in the vicinity. A. has seven annual fairs; is the seat of a tribunal, a communal college, and a normal school, and has a chamber of commerce. It is the seat of the archbishopric of Tarn, comprising the suffragan bishoprics of Rodez, Cahors, Mende, and Perpignan. It is the birth-place of the unfortunate circumnavigator La Peyrouse, to whose memory a bronze statue was erected here in 1844.—The canton of A. comprises 16 communes, and had a pop. of 21,980 in 1831.—The arrond. of A. contains 566 sq. m., and comprises the cantons of Alban, Albi, Monestiés, Pampelonne, Realmont, Valderies, Valence-en-Albigeois, and Villefranche. Its pop. in 1836 was 84,929; in 1841, 86,817.
ALCAÇAR (Point), a high and steep projecting cliff on the coast of Barbary, 91 m. E of Malabat.

ALCAÇAR KEBIR, a town of Marocco, in the prov. of El Garb, seated on the S bank of the Lucos, here a deep and rapid stream flowing W 4 NW, 20 m. from the coast, and 50 m. SW by S from Tetuan; in N lat. 35° 1', W long. 5° 49'. Gräberg estimates the pop. at 6,000. The town contains ten mosques, and is environed with walled gardens and orchards. The surrounding country is a flat tract of rich meadow land. Ali Bey describes it as larger than Tangiers. Colonel Keatinge says it is "a considerable but not a populous place, dirty, straggling, and dilapidated." Near this town was fought the battle in which Don Sebastian, king of Portugal, was defeated and slain, with the flower of his chivalry, by the Moors, in 1578.
ALCAÇAR DE SAN JUAN, a v. of Spain, in the prov. of New Castile, 43 m. NE of Ciudad Real, on a tongue of land formed by the junction of the Zaneara and the Rianzares.

ALCAÇAR DO SAL, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the Caldao river, 36 m. ESE of Setuval; in the midst of a barren district abounding in salt marshes, and of which the chief produce is salt and rushes. Pop. 4,000.
ALCALA', a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 18 m. NW by W of Montemor.

ALCALA'-DE-CHISBERT, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 24 m. NE by N of Castellon. The streets are "hilly, narrow, and winding; the houses low and disagreeable." [Laborde.] It has a fine church, built in the last cent. Pop. 3,600.
ALCALA'-DE-LOS-GAZULES, a small town of Spain, in Andalusia, 6 m. NE of Medina-Sidonia.

ALCALA'-DE-GUADAIRA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, 5 m. E by S of Seville. Pop. 1,800, who are chiefly employed in manufacturing bread, for | Portugal by Braganza. Pop. 600. ALC which this town—sometimes called A. de los Panaderos, i. e. 'A. of the Bakers'-has long been famous. The olives of A. are likewise celebrated. It has a fine Moorish castle. The hills which surround the town are filled with springs of excellent water; and the city of Seville is supplied by an aqueduct, which, commencing near A., is conducted in an open canal on the top of arches 12 ft. in diam. and supported by pillars. It appears to be of mixed Roman and Moorish architecture.

ALCALA'-DE-HENARES, an ancient city of Spain, in the prov. of New Castile, in N lat. 40° 281, W long. 3° 25', about 16 m. ENE of Madrid, and 42 m. NE of Toledo. It is situated at the extremity of an extensive and elevated plain, near the base of a semicircular range of hills, on the r. bank of the river Nares or Henares, from which its present name is derived. Though large, it is not populous in proportion to its extent, but is "almost wholly taken up by churches, convents, and colleges." It is encircled by a strong wall flanked with square towers; the streets, although badly laid out, are broad, and the houses are well-built, but the general aspect of the city is that of monastic gloom. Among the principal buildings are the cathedral, a fine Gothic structure, and three parish-churches, all of which are adorned with paintings by the best Spanish masters, several convents, four hospitals, a military school, several colleges, and the still unfinished palace of the archbishops of Toledo. But A. was chiefly celebrated for its university, which was founded in 1253 by Sancho, king of Castile, and which, next to that of Salamanca, was the most eminent in Spain. It was richly endowed by Cardinal Ximenes, and under his auspices produced the famous Complutensian polyglott version of the Holy Scriptures, which is said to have cost 52,000 ducats. The removal of the university to Madrid has completed the ruin of this place. It is still, however, the see of a bishop, suffragan of Toledo. In the outskirts of the city is a spring of water, which, from the excellence of its qualities has been constituted a royal monopoly. The environs are pleasant, fertile, and well-cultivated; but the elevated site of the city, at 2,070 ft. above sea-level, renders the climate cold in winter: in summer it is generally mild and genial. A. possesses a considerably active trade, and has some powder-mills and tanneries. The pop. is estimated at 5,000.—A. is generally identified with the Complutum of the Romans. It was the birth-place of the historian Antonio Solis, the naturalist Bustamente de la Camera, and the world-renowned Cervantes.

ALCALA'-LA-REAL, a city of Spain, in Andalusia, on the frontier of Granada, 30 m. SW of Jaen. It is a picturesque town, built on a conical hill, at an elevation of 2,867 ft. above sea-level, in the midst of a mountainous country. Pop. 9,000. Having been taken in 1340 from the Moors by Alonso XI. in person, it received its appellative Real in memory of this circumstance. The French general Sebastiani defeated the Spaniards under Areizaga and Freire at this place on Jan. 28, 1810.

ALCAMO, a district and town of Sicily, on the gulf of Castellamare, in the prov. of Mazzara. The town is finely situated at the foot of Mount Santo Bonifato, 27 m. WSW of Palermo, and "presents an appearance of neatness." [Hoare.] In its vicinity are the ruins of Segeste, long the rival of Selinunte.
ALCANADRE, a river of Spain in Aragon, rising in the Guara mountains, and flowing in a S by W course to its junction with the Isuela, whence the united stream flows SE to the Cinca.
ALCANICES, or ALCANZAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, on the highroad from Zamora into

ALCANIZ, a town of Spain, in Aragon, on the r. bank of the Bergantes, 50 m. SSW. of Lerida. Pop. 5,000. Alum is wrought in the vicinity. The French general Vattier, with 500 men, defeated 4,000 Spaniards under Areizaga, in the neighbourhood of this town, on 26th Jan., 1809.

ALCA'NTARA, a fortified city of Spain, in Estremadura, on the frontier of Portugal, in N lat. 39° 44', 230 m. by road WSW of Madrid. Pop. 3,000. It is built on an eminence on the S bank of the Tagus, over which there is here a beautiful Roman bridge of 6 arches. It was the Lancia of the Vettones, the Norba Casarea of the Romans, and the Al- Kantarah, i. e. 'the Bridge,' of the Moors. El Puente de A. was built A. D. 105. It is 600 ft. long, by 28 ft. wide; and has an elevation of 245 ft. above the level of the Tagus, which here rolls its flood 40 ft. deep through a wild rocky gorge. One arch was restored by Charles V. in 1543; another, the second on the r. bank, having been blown up by the British in the campaign of 1809, was repaired by them in 1812. The military and religious order of the knights of A. originated in a small body of knights from Salamanca, who successfully held the passage of the Tagus at this place against the Moors in the 12th cent. ALCANTARA, [i.e. in Arabic, 'the Bridge,'] a fertile but not picturesque valley, lying between Bellas and the city of Lisbon, which receives its name from a stupendous aqueduct, one of the most magnificent works of modern times, 56,380 ft. long, by which the capital is supplied with water. The springs which feed it come from Ribeira de Carenque, Cannessas, and other adjacent places. It crosses the valley on 35 arches, of which the central one has a height of 249 ft, according to Bowles; 212 according to Bell; or 220 according to Rennie. Its width is 150 ft. according to Bowles; 104 ft. 9 in. according to Bell; and 108 ft. 2 in. according to Rennie. Fourteen of the arches are pointed; the rest are semicircular. A noble pathway, bordered by a parapet of solid blocks of stone, leads across the summit; the water flowing under an arched stone roof in a stream 13 inches wide by 7 deep, for which there are two channels, only one of which is used at a time. Light and air are admitted at intervals into the vaulting by windows and towers, which are furnished with close iron gratings. The breadth above the buttresses is 23 ft. 3 in.; the width of the section at the top is 30 ft. 1 in.; at bottom 25 ft. 10 in. The water has a fine clear appearance, but holds a large portion of carbonate of lime in solution. The foundation stone of this great work was laid in 1713, under the auspices of John V., by a Neapolitan named Canavarro, who, dying during the progress of the work, was succeeded by Brigadier Mansel de Maya, who finished it on 6th Aug., 1732.—Semple.—Rennie.

ALCA'NTARA, a river of Sicily, rising in the mountains of Messina, and flowing about 50 m. in an E and SE course, throughout which it separates the prov. of Messina from that of Catania. It is the ancient Onobalas.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranham, on the W side of San Marcos bay. It has a good harbour for coasting vessels. Pop. 5,000.
ALCA'NTARA (SAN PEDRO DE), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, on the l. bank of the Ilheos, 135 m. SSW of Bahia.
ALCANTARILLA, a town of Spain, 5 m. S by W of Murcia.

ALCARAZ, a town of Spain, in New Castile, 45 m. SW by W of Albacete, at the foot of a chain of mountains to which it gives name, and on the l. bank of the Guadarmena. Pop. 3,400.—Also a town of Catalonia, 6 m. SW of Lerida, supposed by some to be the Orcia of Ptolemy. Laborde says, two blocks of freestone about half-a-mile from this town mark the bounds between Catalonia and Aragon.
ALCARRIA, a district of Spain, in New Castile, lying to the S of the Mountains of Cuença and La Mancha. It is a pastoral country, and rich in honey, game, fish, and olives.

ALCATRASSES, or Cormorant Isles, a group of rocky islets off the E coast of Brazil, of which the principal one is in S lat. 24° 6', W long. 45° 38'. These rocks ought not to be approached within 4 m., unless with a fair wind.
ALCATRAZ, a barren volcanic islet on the W coast ALC of Africa, near the mouth of the Rio Nunez, in N lat. 10° 37′, W long. 15° 20′; 25 m. SE from Pullam island.
ALCAUDETE, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, 23 m. WSW of Jaen. Alt. above sea-level 2,290 ft.
ALCESTE, an island in the Yellow sea, in N lat. 33° 56′, E long. 125°.

ALCESTER, a parish and town of England, in Warwickshire, at the confluence of the Alne and the Arrow. Area of the p. 1,530 acres. Pop. 2,399. The town-which some antiquarians identify with the Alana of Richard of Circnester—is 16 m. WSW of Warwick. Its principal manufacture is that of needles. The name is commonly pronounced in Warwickshire Aulster or Auster.
